title: Modern Management of the Patient With Paget Disease of Bone

items.title: Why Paget disease of bone remains clinically important
items.text: PDB is the second most common metabolic bone disease after osteoporosis, typically identified by elevated alkaline phosphatase or incidental radiographs. Most patients are asymptomatic, but bone pain and local complications drive therapy decisions.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "PDB is the second most common metabolic bone disease after osteoporosis."

items.title: How bisphosphonates help painful Paget disease
items.text: Bisphosphonates—especially intravenous zoledronic acid—are more than three times more likely than placebo to eliminate bone pain, with a number needed to treat of only 5; patients with shorter symptom duration respond better.
items.reference: Therapeutic Management: "bisphosphonate treatment is more than 3 times more likely than placebo to eliminate bone pain in PDB (31% vs 9% [relative risk (RR) = 3.42; 95% confidence interval (CI), 1.31-8.90])"

items.title: Why expectant management is advised for asymptomatic Paget disease
items.text: There is insufficient evidence that bisphosphonates or other interventions prevent deformity, fractures, orthopedic surgery, or hearing loss in asymptomatic patients—treat pain, not isolated biochemical elevation.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "there is insufficient evidence of other disease benefit (ie, prevention of deformity, fractures, orthopedic surgery, hearing loss) with bisphosphonates or other pharmacologic/nonpharmacologic interventions, such that expectant management and treatment of patients with PDB who have been pain is currently advised."

items.title: How to stage imaging after biochemical suspicion of PDB
items.text: Plain radiographs of symptomatic or high-yield sites are reasonable first steps; whole-body technetium-99m bone scintigraphy is more sensitive than plain films and is recommended to define the extent of metabolically active disease.
items.reference: Diagnosis: "whole-body bone scintigraphy is recommended as well, in addition to targeted radiographs, as a means to fully and accurately define the extent of metabolically active disease in patients with PDB."

items.title: Pitfall: biochemical relapse does not equal clinical relapse
items.text: After zoledronic acid, alkaline phosphatase may rise without return of pain—PRISM trials showed intensive treat-to-target normalization did not reduce pain, fractures, or orthopedic procedures and may increase fracture risk in older, long-standing disease.
items.reference: Barriers to Optimal Practice: "Biochemical relapse (ie, increase in alkaline phosphatase above the normal range) does not necessarily predict clinical relapse (ie, recurrence of bone pain), thereby presenting a challenge in the long-term management of patients with PDB."

items.title: Zoledronic acid versus oral risedronate in painful PDB
items.text: Head-to-head data favor IV zoledronic acid 5 mg over oral risedronate for pain relief and lower clinical relapse rates; calcitonin is second-line when bisphosphonates are contraindicated; denosumab is not recommended.
items.reference: Therapeutic Management: "zoledronic acid, 5 mg intravenously, is superior to risedronate, 30 mg orally for 2 months, in relieving bone pain (RR, 1.36; 95% CI, 1.06-1.74)."

items.title: Why bone-specific alkaline phosphatase helps when total ALP is borderline
items.text: Total alkaline phosphatase reflects hepatic and skeletal fractions; patients with PDB may have normal totals when hepatic contribution is low or skeletal involvement is limited—bone-specific ALP, P1NP, or CTX can confirm elevated turnover.
items.reference: Barriers to Optimal Practice: "PDB may be underrecognized in patients with an alkaline phosphatase level within but at the upper limit of the normal range, in which case measurement of bone-specific alkaline phosphatase and/or other bone biomarkers (P1NP, C-telopeptide) may be helpful."

items.title: How to manage asymptomatic calvarial Paget disease
items.text: Hearing impairment is significantly more common than in the general population even without subjective symptoms—audiometry is indicated; bisphosphonates have not been shown to slow or reverse hearing loss.
items.reference: Case 3: "the incidence of hearing impairment in patients with PDB is significantly higher than in the general population."

items.title: Paget disease pathobiology: lytic then sclerotic remodeling
items.text: Initial osteoclastic lytic lesions recruit accelerated osteoblastic woven bone formation, yielding hypervascular, structurally inferior bone that may later "burn out" into sclerotic inactive lesions.
items.reference: Prevalence and Pathogenesis: "The result of this process is an enlarged, hypervascular and structurally inferior skeletal site."

items.title: SQSTM1 variants and familial Paget disease
items.text: Up to 30% of patients have a positive family history; SQSTM1 pathogenic variants account for ~21% in some cohorts and associate with earlier, more severe disease including spinal stenosis.
items.reference: Prevalence and Pathogenesis: "Within the United States, studies suggest that a first-degree relative of a patient with PDB is 7 times more likely to develop the disease than someone with an unaffected relative."

items.title: How to extend staging in incidentally discovered pelvic Paget disease
items.text: Asymptomatic patients with focal pelvic disease may have multifocal involvement including spine—whole-body bone scan is indicated before reassurance or therapy; bisphosphonates should not start without defining disease extent.
items.reference: Case 1: "whole-body bone scanning with technetium $^{99}$Tc (Answer C) is indicated."

items.title: Surveillance after successful zoledronic acid in monostotic PDB
items.text: Rising alkaline phosphatase without pain warrants clinical and biochemical surveillance every 6–12 months—not automatic retreatment or repeat scintigraphy when the diagnosis and monostotic site are already established.
items.reference: Case 5: "continued clinical and biochemical surveillance (Answer C) is indicated for this patient."

items.question: A 64-year-old man has pelvic CT showing iliac and pubic thickening, ALP 114 U/L (24–110), no pain, and normal hip exam. What is the best next step?
options: Zoledronic acid 5 mg IV
Alendronate 40 mg daily for 6 months
Technetium-99m whole-body bone scintigraphy
Fasting serum C-telopeptide
items.explanation: Asymptomatic PDB with possible multifocal disease requires bone scintigraphy to define extent before treatment or reassurance.
items.reference: Case 1: "whole-body bone scanning with technetium $^{99}$Tc (Answer C) is indicated."

items.question: A 52-year-old man has active symptomatic pelvic PDB on radiograph and bone scan with ALP 114 U/L and bone-specific ALP 33 µg/L. What is the best treatment?
options: Zoledronic acid 5 mg IV
Risedronate 30 mg daily for 2 months
Calcitonin 100 units SC three times weekly
Denosumab 60 mg SC every 6 months
items.explanation: Symptomatic active PDB with recent pain is best treated with IV zoledronic acid, superior to oral risedronate in trials.
items.reference: Case 2: "Zoledronic acid intravenously (Answer A) is superior to oral risedronate (Answer B) based on a head-to-head comparison and is the best treatment for this patient."

items.question: A 60-year-old woman has skull Paget on bone scan with mild calvarial thickening, no headaches, and denies hearing loss. Best next step?
options: Zoledronic acid 5 mg IV
Alendronate 40 mg daily for 6 months
Audiometry testing
CT of the skull
items.explanation: Subclinical hearing loss is common in skull PDB; audiometry guides assistive technology—bisphosphonates do not reverse hearing loss.
items.reference: Case 3: "audiometry testing (Answer C) is indicated to determine whether hearing loss is present"

items.question: Which statement about bisphosphonate therapy in asymptomatic Paget disease is most accurate?
options: IV zoledronic acid prevents hearing loss in all skull lesions
Intensive treat-to-target ALP normalization reduces fracture risk in all ages
Robust RCT evidence supports bisphosphonates only for reducing skeletal pain
Denosumab is first-line when bisphosphonates fail
items.explanation: Cochrane data robustly support pain reduction; other outcomes lack evidence; denosumab is not recommended.
items.reference: Therapeutic Management: "existing evidence for bisphosphonate use is robust only for reduction in skeletal pain."

items.question: The PRISM and PRISM-EZ trials demonstrated that intensive bisphosphonate treatment in PDB:
options: Reduced bone pain more than symptom-driven therapy over ~3 years
Did not reduce bone pain, fractures, or orthopedic surgery vs symptomatic treatment
Normalized ALP and eliminated all long-term fracture risk
Should be standard for all patients under age 50
items.explanation: Intensive therapy did not improve pain, fractures, or surgery needs; long-term ALP normalization did not improve quality of life and may raise fracture risk in older cohorts.
items.reference: Therapeutic Management: "intensive bisphosphonate treatment did not reduce bone pain, fractures, or need for orthopedic surgery compared with symptom-driven treatment in patients with PDB over an average of 3 years."

items.question: When plain radiographs are negative but PDB is suspected biochemically, the recommended next imaging step is:
options: MRI of the pelvis only
Whole-body bone scintigraphy
PET-CT of the spine
No further imaging; repeat ALP in 6 months
items.explanation: Scintigraphy is more sensitive than plain films for detecting metabolically active PDB.
items.reference: Diagnosis: "In the absence of plain radiographic involvement, whole-body bone scintigraphy is recommended based on its superior sensitivity for detecting PDB compared with the sensitivity of plain x-rays"

items.question: In a patient with established painful PDB on imaging, fasting C-telopeptide measurement is:
options: Required before starting zoledronic acid
Superior to ALP for diagnosis
Of limited utility for diagnosis or therapeutic management
Mandatory to predict clinical relapse after zoledronic acid
items.explanation: Turnover markers have limited utility once diagnosis and activity are established; they do not reliably predict clinical relapse after zoledronic acid.
items.reference: Case 2: "measurement of bone turnover (Answer D) has limited utility in either the diagnosis or therapeutic management of this patient."

items.question: Which complication of Paget disease is correctly paired with its mechanism?
options: High-output heart failure from hypervascular pagetic bone (rare)
Premature mortality from osteosarcoma in most patients
Hearing loss only after skull deformity is visible
Fractures occur exclusively in burned-out sclerotic lesions
items.explanation: Hypervascular pagetic bone can rarely cause high-output failure; osteosarcoma is <1% without excess mortality; hearing loss can be subclinical.
items.reference: Clinical Presentation: "Hypervascularity of affected bone may exacerbate bleeding risk with orthopedic procedures that involve pagetic sites and has been associated, rarely, with high-output cardiac failure."

items.question: Paget disease of bone epidemiology is best described by which statement?
options: Equal prevalence in Asian and European ancestry
More common in females than males
Prevalence roughly doubles each decade after age 50
Incidence is rising sharply worldwide
items.explanation: PDB affects ~1–3% of older adults, favors Anglo-Saxon/European heritage and males (~1.4:1), and prevalence appears to be declining.
items.reference: Prevalence and Pathogenesis: "it roughly doubles in incidence for each decade after age 50 years."

items.question: A patient with painful PDB inadequately controlled medically requires hip replacement. Which is true?
options: Bisphosphonates are proven to eliminate perioperative bleeding risk
Hip or knee arthroplasty is recommended when medical therapy is inadequate
Surgery is contraindicated in all polyostotic disease
Osteotomy is never considered for deformity
items.explanation: Insufficient evidence supports pharmacologic benefit on orthopedic outcomes, but arthroplasty is recommended when medical therapy fails; osteotomy may be considered for deformity.
items.reference: Therapeutic Management: "hip or knee arthroplasty is recommended in patients in whom medical therapy is inadequate."

items.question: A woman with prior monostotic femoral PDB treated with zoledronic acid has normalized ALP historically but now ALP 140 U/L without pain. Best management?
options: Repeat zoledronic acid 5 mg IV
Switch to alendronate 40 mg daily
Clinical and biochemical surveillance every 6–12 months
Repeat whole-body bone scintigraphy
items.explanation: Biochemical relapse without pain does not mandate retreatment or repeat scintigraphy in known monostotic PDB.
items.reference: Case 5: "continued clinical and biochemical surveillance (Answer C) is indicated for this patient."

items.question: The initial pagetic bone lesion is best characterized as:
options: Purely sclerotic with low osteoclast activity
Lytic with increased osteoclast number and activity
Malignant osteoblastic proliferation
Avascular necrosis of trabecular bone
items.explanation: The initial lesion is lytic from increased osteoclast activity, followed by accelerated osteoblastic woven bone formation.
items.reference: Prevalence and Pathogenesis: "The initial lesion in PDB, which may be solitary (mono-ostotic) or multicentric (polyostotic), is lytic in nature and due to increased number and activity of osteoclasts."

items.question: In a patient without localizing symptoms but elevated ALP suggesting PDB, a reasonable limited radiographic survey includes:
options: Skull, abdomen/pelvis, and tibia
Hands and feet only
Cervical spine only
No radiographs; proceed directly to biopsy
items.explanation: Limited survey of skull/facial bones, abdomen (pelvis/proximal femurs), and tibia is recommended given high prevalence at these sites.
items.reference: Diagnosis: "a limited x-ray survey of the skull and facial bones, abdomen (which includes the pelvis and proximal femurs), and tibia is recommended given the high prevalence of involvement of these sites."

items.statement: Intravenous bisphosphonates confer a greater and more sustained analgesic effect than oral bisphosphonates in painful Paget disease.
items.explanation: Main conclusions and RCT data support superior pain relief with IV therapy.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "Painful PDB is responsive to treatment with bisphosphonates, with a greater and more sustained effect conferred by intravenous than oral therapy."

items.statement: Bisphosphonate treatment is proven to prevent hearing loss in asymptomatic skull Paget disease.
items.explanation: There is insufficient evidence that bisphosphonates prevent hearing loss.
items.reference: Case 3: "There is no evidence to date that treatment with bisphosphonates (Answers A and B) slows or reverses hearing loss in patients with PDB."

items.statement: Lack of biochemical relapse after zoledronic acid reliably identifies patients at risk of clinical relapse on risedronate but not zoledronic acid.
items.explanation: For zoledronic acid, lack of biochemical relapse was not helpful in defining clinical relapse risk.
items.reference: Therapeutic Management: "lack of biochemical relapse (increase in alkaline phosphatase level) was not helpful in defining which patients were at risk of clinical relapse when treated with zoledronic acid."

items.statement: Malignant transformation to osteosarcoma occurs in more than 10% of Paget patients.
items.explanation: Osteosarcoma transformation is very rare (<1%).
items.reference: Clinical Presentation: "Very rarely, malignant transformation to osteosarcoma (<1%) or giant-cell tumor development occurs."

items.statement: Denosumab is recommended for Paget disease when bisphosphonates fail.
items.explanation: Denosumab is not recommended because of insufficient evidence.
items.reference: Therapeutic Management: "denosumab is not recommended for PDB because of insufficient evidence."

items.statement: A first-degree relative of a PDB patient is about seven times more likely to develop PDB than someone with an unaffected relative.
items.explanation: Familial clustering is well documented in US cohorts.
items.reference: Prevalence and Pathogenesis: "a first-degree relative of a patient with PDB is 7 times more likely to develop the disease than someone with an unaffected relative."

items.statement: Whole-body bone scintigraphy may be negative at sites of inactive Paget disease.
items.explanation: Inactive burned-out lesions may not uptake tracer.
items.reference: Diagnosis: "whole-body bone scintigraphy may be negative at sites of inactive PDB."

items.statement: Long-term intensive ALP normalization over more than 7 years improved quality of life in PRISM-EZ.
items.explanation: Long-term normalization did not improve pain, quality of life, or orthopedic procedures and may increase fracture risk.
items.reference: Therapeutic Management: "long-term normalization of alkaline phosphatase over more than 7 years did not improve bone pain, improve quality of life, or reduce the need for orthopedic procedures, and it may actually be associated with a higher fracture risk."

items.assertion: Assertion: A patient with recent-onset Paget bone pain is an appropriate candidate for bisphosphonate therapy.
items.reason: Reason: Bisphosphonates are proven to prevent all orthopedic and neurologic complications of Paget disease.
items.explanation: Both true, but R is false—evidence supports pain relief, not proven prevention of other outcomes.
items.reference: Barriers to Optimal Practice: "bisphosphonate treatment of patients with PDB has not been proven to reduce the risk for orthopedic and neurologic outcomes."

items.assertion: Assertion: An asymptomatic patient with biochemical and radiographic PDB should receive zoledronic acid immediately.
items.reason: Reason: Without treatment, multifocal spine disease will invariably cause neurologic complications.
items.explanation: Neither is supported—expectant management is advised absent pain; neurologic benefit unproven.
items.reference: Case 1: "there is not an indication to treat him with pharmacologic therapy, including bisphosphonates."

items.assertion: Assertion: Rising alkaline phosphatase after prior zoledronic acid always requires retreatment.
items.reason: Reason: Biochemical relapse does not necessarily predict clinical relapse.
items.explanation: A is false when pain is absent; R is true.
items.reference: Barriers to Optimal Practice: "Biochemical relapse (ie, increase in alkaline phosphatase above the normal range) does not necessarily predict clinical relapse (ie, recurrence of bone pain)"

items.assertion: Assertion: Audiometry is indicated in patients with skull Paget disease even without subjective hearing loss.
items.reason: Reason: Hearing impairment prevalence is significantly higher than in the general population.
items.explanation: Both true and causally linked.
items.reference: Case 3: "the incidence of hearing impairment in patients with PDB is significantly higher than in the general population."

items.assertion: Assertion: Zoledronic acid is superior to risedronate for pain relief in PDB.
items.reason: Reason: Patients treated with zoledronic acid are less likely to experience clinical relapse than those treated with risedronate.
items.explanation: Both true; head-to-head trial supports superiority for pain and relapse.
items.reference: Therapeutic Management: "Patients treated with zoledronic acid are also less likely than those treated with risedronate to experience clinical relapse (recurrence of bone pain) (9.2% vs 25.2%)"

items.assertion: Assertion: Whole-body scintigraphy is indicated in a newly diagnosed asymptomatic patient with pelvic PDB on CT.
items.reason: Reason: Multifocal disease including spine may exist and influence management decisions.
items.explanation: Both true—the case vignette links extent assessment to treatment candidacy.
items.reference: Case 1: "he may have multifocal disease that includes the spine, which could position him as a candidate for treatment because of concerns regarding disease progression and possible neurologic complications."

items.assertion: Assertion: Calcitonin may improve bone pain when bisphosphonates are contraindicated.
items.reason: Reason: Calcitonin is superior to zoledronic acid in all PDB patients.
items.explanation: Assertion has low-level support; reason is false.
items.reference: Therapeutic Management: "Low level evidence indicates that calcitonin may improve bone pain in patients with PDB, although it may be considered when bisphosphonate use is contraindicated."

items.assertion: Assertion: A normal total alkaline phosphatase excludes Paget disease.
items.reason: Reason: Low hepatic fraction or limited skeletal involvement can yield normal total ALP despite PDB.
items.explanation: A is false; R correctly explains why.
items.reference: Diagnosis: "a low hepatic fraction may result in a normal total alkaline phosphatase value."
